html:
Johan August Strindberg, born January 22, 1849, in Stockholm, and died May 14, 1912, in Stockholm, was a Swedish author. For four decades, Strindberg was a prominent figure in the literary landscape of Sweden. He was often controversial and frequently involved in personal conflicts. Among his works are numerous novels, short stories, and plays that are considered classics of Swedish literature, and he is regarded as one of Sweden's most important authors. Internationally, however, he is primarily known as a playwright. He also worked as a visual artist, painting and taking photographs, although this aspect of his work has mainly been recognized by later generations.

Strindberg was very prolific, despite periods of inactivity, and groundbreaking in several genres. He is generally considered an innovator of the Swedish language. Strindberg belonged to the group known as the "eighties generation" and was partially involved in issues related to the emerging labor movement. He also dedicated himself, at times, to independent and idiosyncratic research in natural sciences and occultism.

== Biography ==

Johan August Strindberg was born in the building of the Sundhetskollegiet on Riddarholmen in Stockholm on January 22, 1849. He was the third of eight children from his father's first marriage. After the demolition of his birthplace, Strindberg named the play "Brända Tomten" (The Burnt Plot). In 1851, the family moved to Klarakvarteren and in 1856 to Norrtullsgatan 14.

Strindberg's father, Carl Oscar Strindberg, was a steamboat commissioner responsible for freight traffic on Lake Mälaren and the Göta Canal. His mother, Eleonora Ulrika Norling, had previously worked as a waitress at Liljeholmens guesthouse. Before her marriage to Carl Oscar, she worked as a housekeeper for him; hence the title "The Servant Girl's Son," although the home was not as proletarian as Strindberg portrayed it. Carl Oscar married her in 1847. Her piety had a strong influence on the author. Strindberg's aunt, Lisette Strindberg, was married to Samuel Owen, a steamboat pioneer.

In "The Servant Girl's Son," Strindberg describes the furniture in their home as consisting mainly of "cradles and beds. Children lay on ironing boards and chairs." However, due to the high infant mortality rate at the time, there was a constant cycle of "christenings, funerals, christenings, funerals. Sometimes two christenings without an intervening funeral."

As a child, Strindberg was quiet and reserved. His father was strict, and Strindberg grew up in a home characterized by discipline, piety, and cultural interests. During his childhood, he devoted himself to reading, studying natural sciences, and cultivating flowers. August Strindberg was a very sensitive and fragile child. He "feared the fists of his brothers, the braids of the maids, his grandmother's canes, his mother's scoldings, and his father's birch rod." He summarized his impressions of his siblings with the words: "four oppressive brothers and some screaming siblings – he could not remember whether they were boys or girls, or how many there were." Even at the age of seven or eight, Strindberg had suicidal thoughts, according to his own account.

In 1856, he began attending Klara school. On December 30, 1861, Strindberg wrote a letter about his friend Flodcrantz, who fell through the ice on Brunnsviken and drowned. On the night of March 19-20, 1862, his mother died, and he wrote a letter to his brother Oscar about it on March 22. Strindberg had a difficult relationship with his father, which worsened after his mother's death. The following year, his father remarried Emilia Charlotta Peterson, who was thirty years younger and the family's twenty-two-year-old housekeeper (housemaid), whom Strindberg came to despise. However, later interpretations have portrayed Emilia as fulfilling the role of a stepmother, especially for August's sisters.

Strindberg passed his matriculation examination at the private school Stockholms lyceum in 1867. After completing his schooling, he began studying at Uppsala University but dropped out after only one semester because he was unhappy and lacked money. He then supported himself for a time as a primary school teacher and later as a tutor while studying to become a doctor. He then tried to become an actor and worked as an extra at the Dramatic Theatre, but in 1870, he decided to return to Uppsala. It was during this time that Strindberg began his writing career. His first published work is the play "Fritänkaren" (The Freethinker), written in 1869 and printed in 1870 under the pseudonym Härver Ulf, which was never performed. Strindberg portrays himself as a rather dissolute young man during his student days, but his classmates testified that he actually worked like a horse from five in the morning and lived simply. He was described as a friendly and cheerful companion, but he also had periods of deep depression when he wanted to take his own life.

His academic career was hindered by his habit of always disagreeing with and arguing with the teachers, and he dropped out of university without graduating. His writing talent was considered promising and recognized by King Charles XV, who awarded him a scholarship in 1871 for future university studies. In the collection of short stories "From Fjärdingen and Svartbäcken" (1877), he portrays memories of Uppsala in a satirical and pessimistic manner. After two years...

How certain are we about this?

Information about people from the 20th century is extremely well-documented. Uncertainty rarely concerns facts, but rather interpretations and evaluations, which can change over time and depending on the observer.
tldr: August Strindberg was a highly significant Swedish author who wrote famous novels and plays. He is one of the most important figures in Swedish literary history.
lattlast:
August Strindberg was born in Stockholm in 1849 and became one of Sweden's most famous authors. He wrote many books that are now considered classics, meaning they remain highly important and are frequently read. He was also interested in art and natural sciences.

Strindberg had a difficult childhood in a large family where discipline was often strict. After his mother died when he was young, he developed a strained relationship with his father. He studied at the university but dropped out because he didn't enjoy it and was short on money.

Before becoming known as an author, he worked as a teacher and tried to become an actor. Despite frequently getting into arguments and conflicts with others, his talent was recognized by King Karl XV, who provided him with financial support for his studies.
